coredata.dc:title: Engineering efficient artificial nanozyme based on chitosan grafted Fe-doped-carbon dots for bacteria biofilm eradication
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Ting
Chen, Huanhuan
Gao, Xiang
Wu, Zeyu
Ye, Yingwang
Shen, Yizhong
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial biofilms have evoked worldwide attention owing to their serious threats to public health, but how to effectively eliminate bacterial biofilms still remains great challenges. Here, we rationally designed a novel and vigorous chitosan grafted Fe-doped-carbon dots (CS@Fe/CDs) as an efficient artificial nanozyme to combat rigid bacterial biofilms through the selective activation of Fenton-like reaction-triggered peroxidase-like catalytic activity and the synergistic antibacterial activity of CS. On the one hand, the peroxidase-like catalytic activity made CS@Fe/CDs catalyze H2O2 for producing hydroxyl radicals (•OH), resulting in efficient cleavage of extracellular DNA (eDNA). On the other hand, CS was capable of binding with the negatively charged cell membrane through electrostatic interaction, changing the cell membrane permeability and causing cell death within bacterial biofilms. Based on their synergistic effects, the fragments of bacterial biofilm and exposed bacteria were persistently eradicated. Remarkably, CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme not only enabled the effective destroying of gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) biofilms, but also completely eliminated gram-negative Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) biofilms, showing great potential as a promising anti-biofilm agent against bacteria biofilms. This proposed synergistic strategy for bacterial biofilm eradication might offer a powerful modality to manage of bacterial biofilm fouling in food safety and environmental protection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: General design of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme for bacterial biofilm elimination. (a) Schematic illustration for one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme. (b) Schematic diagram for the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme against bacteria. (c) Schematic diagram for CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme to bacterial biofilm elimination.

Characterization of CS@Fe/CDs. (a) TEM image of CS@Fe/CDs. Inset: the lattice fringes structure of CS@Fe/CDs. (b) TEM image of free Fe doping CS@CDs. (c) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of CS@Fe/CDs and CS@CDs in D.I. water. (d) XRD patterns of CS@Fe/CDs and CS@CDs. (e) Zeta potentials of CS@Fe/CDs and CS@CDs in D.I. water. (f) XPS analysis of CS@Fe/CDs and CS@CDs. (g) Fe 2p spectra of CS@Fe/CDs. (h) N 1 s spectra of CS@CDs and CS@Fe/CDs. (i) Absorption spectra of CS@CDs and CS@Fe/CDs in D.I. water at the same concentration of 50.0 μg·mL−1.

Characterization of CS@Fe/CDs. (a) FT-IR spectra of CS, CS@CDs, CS@Fe/CDs and CDs, respectively. (b) Excitation and emission spectra of CS@Fe/CDs in D.I. water and the absorption spectrum of CS@Fe/CDs. Inset: (left) the photograph of CS@Fe/CDs in D.I. water and (right) fluorescence image of CS@Fe/CDs under a 365 nm UV lamp. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CS@Fe/CDs in D.I. water under different excitation wavelength.

Profiling the peroxidase mimic activity of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme. (a) Absorption spectra of (1) H2O2 + TMB, (2) CS@Fe/CDs + TMB, (3) CS@Fe/CDs + H2O2, (4) CS@CDs + H2O2 + TMB, and (5) CS@Fe/CDs + H2O2 + TMB at room temperature. Inset were the corresponding photographs. Experimental conditions: H2O2, 100.0 μM; TMB, 1.0 mM; CS@Fe/CDs, 50.0 μg·mL−1; incubtion time, 10.0 min (b) Time-dependent absorbance OD value of 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs + 100.0 μM H2O2 + 1.0 mM TMB at ~ 652 nm following incubation with different pH at room temperature for 10.0 min (c) Absorption spectra of 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs + 100.0 μM H2O2 + 1.0 mM TMB at different temperatures. (d) Comparison the fluorescence intensity of TA, TA + H2O2, TA + CS@Fe/CDs, TA + H2O2 + CS@Fe/CDs, and CS@Fe/CDs at ~ 425 nm after incubation at 37.0 ℃ for 12.0 h under dark condition. Experimental conditions: TA, 1.0 mM; CS@Fe/CDs, 50.0 μg·mL−1; H2O2, 1.0 mM. (e) Comparison the absorbance OD value at ~ 665 nm of 5.0 μg·mL−1 MB plus 1.0 mM H2O2 before and after incubation with 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, and 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs at 37.0 ℃ for 30.0 min under dark condition, respectively. (f) EPR spectra of DMPO and DMPO following incubation with CS@Fe/CDs (50.0 μg·mL−1) + H2O2 (1.0 mM).

Exploring the antibacterial activity of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme. (a) Photographs of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa receiving four indicated treatments. (b & c) Quantitative analysis of the relative survival rates of living S. aureus and P. aeruginosa as shown in Fig. 5a. Data expressed mean ± SD (n = 3, *** P < 0.001). (d) TEM images of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa receiving four indicated treatments. Scale bars: 2 µm. (e) Fluorescence images of •OH in living S. aureus and P. aeruginosa receiving four indicated treatments. Scale bars: 50 µm. The concentrations of CS@Fe/CDs and H2O2 were 50.0 μg·mL−1 and 500.0 μM, respectively. All experiments were incubated at 37.0 ℃ for 3.0 h.

Bacterial biofilm eradication using CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme at 37.0 ℃ for 3.0 h. (a) Fluorescence imaging of SYTO-9/PI in S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms treated with four indicated treatments. Scale bars: 50 µm. (b) SEM images of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms with four indicated treatments. Scale bars: 2 µm for 10,000 × . (c) Quantitative analysis of the relative biofilm mass receiving four indicated treatments. The relative biofilm mass = (1 – OD)/ODcontrol × 100%. Data expressed mean ± SD (n = 7, ** P < 0.01). (d & e) Agarose gel electrophoresis results of the DNA inside S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms that treated with four indicated treatments. CS@Fe/CDs, 50.0 μg·mL−1; H2O2, 500.0 μM.

head.ce:title: Engineering efficient artificial nanozyme based on chitosan grafted Fe-doped-carbon dots for bacteria biofilm eradication

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food & Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory for Agricultural Products Processing of Anhui Province, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food & Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory for Agricultural Products Processing of Anhui Province, Hefei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food & Biological Engineering, Key Laboratory for Agricultural Products Processing of Anhui Province, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial biofilms have evoked worldwide attention owing to their serious threats to public health, but how to effectively eliminate bacterial biofilms still remains great challenges. Here, we rationally designed a novel and vigorous chitosan grafted Fe-doped-carbon dots (CS@Fe/CDs) as an efficient artificial nanozyme to combat rigid bacterial biofilms through the selective activation of Fenton-like reaction-triggered peroxidase-like catalytic activity and the synergistic antibacterial activity of CS. On the one hand, the peroxidase-like catalytic activity made CS@Fe/CDs catalyze H2O2 for producing hydroxyl radicals (•OH), resulting in efficient cleavage of extracellular DNA (eDNA). On the other hand, CS was capable of binding with the negatively charged cell membrane through electrostatic interaction, changing the cell membrane permeability and causing cell death within bacterial biofilms. Based on their synergistic effects, the fragments of bacterial biofilm and exposed bacteria were persistently eradicated. Remarkably, CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme not only enabled the effective destroying of gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) biofilms, but also completely eliminated gram-negative Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) biofilms, showing great potential as a promising anti-biofilm agent against bacteria biofilms. This proposed synergistic strategy for bacterial biofilm eradication might offer a powerful modality to manage of bacterial biofilm fouling in food safety and environmental protection.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to antibiotics resistance, bacterial biofilm-triggered microbial contaminations in environmental media (e.g., water or food processing systems) have been considered as one of the most severe threats to public health (Cao et al., 2021; Fan et al., 2021; Mu et al., 2021). It has been reported that the bacterial biofilm represents a special bacterial community, which builds efficient physical and chemical barriers through its self-generated extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) to protect the embedded bacteria, thereby leading to against antibiotics, host immune defenses, as well as environmental stresses (Kumari et al., 2021). In this case, the incomplete elimination of bacterial biofilms in particularly water and food processing environments easily causes persistent microbial contaminations and superbugs (Chen et al., 2016). Moreover, the bacterial biofilms adhered to food and agricultural products-processing equipment or water supply systems may cause food poisoning together with infectious diseases epidemic (Luo et al., 2022). Apart from these, bacterial biofilms are also capable of highly damaging industrial devices, resulting in economic loss (Xie et al., 2020). To address these awkward situations, the key point is to constantly innovate and develop new efficient, economical and eco-friendly anti-biofilm agents.

In the past few years, artificial nanozymes have exhibited a great potential against pathogenic bacteria, because of their merits as easy preparation, low cost, high stability, and multifunctional (Wang et al., 2021a; Zhu et al., 2021). Typically, the artificial nanozymes-based antibacterial agents possess the ability to catalyze the reaction for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS, e.g. •OH), inducing irreversible bacterial damage to realize antibacterial activities (Liang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Unnikrishnana et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2019). Of particular note are the nice membrane permeability and biocompatibility of most artificial nanozymes toward bacteria, which can be capable of avoiding bacterial resistance (Mei et al., 2021). Given such unique properties, vast endeavors have been contributed to develop various artificial nanozymes against pathogenic bacteria and their biofilms, such as metal oxide nanoparticles (Li et al., 2019), carbon-based nanomaterials (Hu et al., 2021; Xi et al., 2019), metal-organic frameworks (Hu et al., 2020b), etc. Although artificial nanozymes have made commendable progress in antibacterial applications, the low binding ability toward bacteria and ROS’s poor diffusivity together with short lifetime can severely limit their disinfection activity (Wei et al., 2021). Recently, as a natural antibacterial agent, chitosan (CS) has attracted extensive attention, due to its good biocompatibility (Wang et al., 2021b), excellent degradability (Bakshi et al., 2020), non-toxic polymer (Hamedi et al., 2022), etc. The currently widely recognized antibacterial mechanism of CS mainly has two aspects. Firstly, CS possesses a positively charged on the surface, which can adsorb to the surface of microorganisms and bind to the negatively charged cell surface through electrostatic interaction, altering cell membrane permeability and causing cell death (Rashki et al., 2021). Moreover, CS can cross the cell membrane and interact with the nucleic acid to interfere with DNA/RNA synthesis and trigger intracellular reactions, leading to cell death (Hosseinnejad and Jafari, 2016). Based on these points, it can be an alternative way to integrate artificial nanozymes with CS for achieving efficient, low-cost, and eco-friendly antibacterial agents, ultimately allowing for bacterial biofilm eradication.

Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, have always attracted widespread interests for applications in traceability and prevention/treatment of environmental microbial contaminations, owing to their advantages of easy and low-cost preparation process, good dispersibility, prominent biocompatibility, controllable structure, etc. (Li et al., 2022; Nayak et al., 2020). In particular, some endeavors have been contributed to explore the nanozymes activities of CDs against pelagic bacteria, giving great potential as CDs-based artificial nanozymes to destroy bacterial biofilms (Yang et al., 2021b). Encouraged by these points above, we here adopted one-pot hydrothermal method to successfully synthesize CS grafted Fe-doped-carbon dots (CS@Fe/CDs), which exhibited an excellent eradication of bacterial biofilms. As a proof of concept, we chose Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, gram-positive bacteria) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa, gram-negative bacteria) as the representative models, whose biofilms possessed a good uptake of CS@Fe/CDs, probably attributable to the binding of CS with the anions on the outer membrane of bacteria (Jeon et al., 2016). In this case, CS@Fe/CDs effectively catalyzed the production of toxic •OH from H2O2, leading to the degradation of extracellular DNA (eDNA), and thus resulting in biofilm eradication ( Fig. 1). This design utilized the synergistic effects of CS targeting bacterial cell membranes and peroxidase-mimicking activity inside CS@Fe/CDs to effectively catalyze •OH generation for efficient biofilm eradication, providing a new strategy for carbon dots-based artificial nanozymes against bacterial biofilms in food safety and environmental protection.

All chemical and biological reagents were acquired from commercial suppliers without further purification. Chitosan (CS, low molecular weight, degree of deacetylation ≥ 95%) and 2-nitrobenzoic acid (DTNB) were obtained by Aladdin Reagent Corporation (Shanghai, China). Ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4.7H2O), citric acid, ethylenediamine, crystal violet (CV), HAc, and H2O2 (30 wt%) were purchased from Sinopharm Holding Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), methylene blue (MB), and disodium terephthalate (TA) were achieved from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HAc-NaAc buffer was bought from Phygene Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Fuzhou, China). Dialysis bag (MWCO = 500–1000 Da) was obtained from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). SYTO-9 green fluorescent nucleic acid stain and Propidium iodide (SYTO-9/PI) were bought from Thermo Scientific (USA). 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was obtained from Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Glutathione reduced (GSH) and Ezup Column Bacteria Genomic DNA Purification Kit were purchased by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Corporation (St Louis, MO, USA). Deionized (DI) water (Millipore, USA, 18.2 MΩ resistivity) was used in all experiments. The S. aureus strain ATCC25923 and P. aeruginosa strain PAO1 were from Guangdong Institute of Microbiology.

An UV-Vis TU-1901 spectrophotometer (PERSEE, Beijing, China) was applied to obtain all absorption spectra. A LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) was conducted to acquire all fluorescence spectra. An inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon, Japan) was used to obtain bacteria imaging. A Field emission transmission electron microscope (JEM-1400 F, Japan) was employed to acquire the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of nanoparticles and bacterial morphology. An ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Electron, USA) was used to characterize the elemental analysis of nanoparticles. A microplate reader (680, Bio-Rad, USA) was applied to record the crystal violet (CV) staining analysis. A Zetasizer (Nano-Z, Malvern, UK) was applied to perform the zeta potentials and hydrodynamic diameters. A Fourier-transform infrared (Perkin Elmer, USA) was used to measure the functional groups. A Bruker EMX-10/12 X-band variable-temperature apparatus was employed to obtain the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra. A Nanodrop 2000 ultra-micro spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to detect the residual levels of DNA inside biofilms. A Gemini500 thermal field scanning electron microscope (ZEISS) was used to obtain the SEM images of bacteria biofilms morphology. A Gel Image System (Tanon 1600) was employed to take images of DNA products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CS@Fe/CDs through one-pot hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CS@Fe/CDs was used one-pot hydrothermal method. In brief, 0.05 g CS, 0.05 g citric acid, 0.13 g FeSO4.7 H2O and 100.0 μL ethylenediamine were added into 5.0 mL D.I. water containing 1% (v/v) HAc. After ultrasound for 30.0 min, the mixture was quickly transferred into a teflon lining reaction kettle and kept at 180.0 ℃ for 6.0 h. After that, the reaction was naturally cooled to room temperature, and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10.0 min. The supernatant was taken out and passed through the 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove impurity. Then the resulting products were dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 24.0 h, and the D.I. water was changed every 6.0 h. The purified CS@Fe/CDs was lyophilized in a freeze-vacuum dryer and stored in a refrigerator at 4.0 ℃ under dark condition. The same synthesis procedure of CS@CDs was performed without adding FeSO4.7 H2O, and the same synthesis procedure of CDs was performed without adding FeSO4.7 H2O and CS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Profiling the peroxidase mimic activity of CS@Fe/CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The classical TMB oxidation-based colorimetric assay was applied to characterize the peroxidase mimic activity of CS@Fe/CDs by measuring its absorbance (OD) value at ~ 652 nm. In a 1.5 mL centrifugal tube, 1.0 mM TMB was respectively incubated with 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs, 100.0 μM H2O2, and 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs + 100.0 μM H2O2 in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.5), respectively. After 10.0 min, the absorbance (OD) value of each sample at ~ 652 nm was determined at room temperature. Meanwhile, the Steady-state kinetic assay was performed by changing the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.2) containing 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs. According to the following Michaelis-Menten equation (Jiang et al., 2018):
1
V

V

Among them, V refers to the initial reaction velocity, K m is the Michaelis constant, [S] represents the substrate concentration, and V max refers to the maximum reaction velocity.

To examine the production of •OH in CS@Fe/CDs to catalyze H2O2, TA oxidation-based fluorescence assay, MB degradtion-based colorimetric assay, and GSH oxidation-based Ellman’s assay were carried out in aqueous solution. For TA oxidation-based fluorescence assay, 1.0 mM TA was incubated without or with 1.0 mM H2O2, 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs, and 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs together with 1.0 mM H2O2 in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.5) at 37.0 °C for 12.0 h. Afterward, the fluorescence spectra of all samples above were gathered with the excitation wavelength of 312 nm, and the fluorescence intensity at ~ 425 nm was used to perform the comparison of •OH levels. Moreover, the MB degradation experiment was also performed to determine the production of •OH. In HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.5), 5.0 μg·mL−1 MB plus 1.0 mM H2O2 were incubated with 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, and 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs at 37.0 ℃ for 30.0 min, respectively. At this point, the absorbance (OD) value of the solution at ~ 665 nm was measured on a UV-Vis absorption spectrophotometer. Besides, GSH oxidation-based Ellman’s assay was further applied to determine •OH levels. Briefly, in a 1.5 mL centrifugal tube, 0.4 mM GSH was incubated without or with 1.0 mM H2O2, 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs, and 1.0 mM H2O2 together with 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.5) at 37.0 °C for 1.0 h, respectively. Subsequently, 0.5 mM 2-nitrobenzoic acid (DTNB) was added into the four solutions to observe the coloration reaction and measure the absorbance (OD) value at ~ 410 nm on a UV-Vis absorption spectrophotometer.

S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were seeded into Luria-Bertani (LB) medium (0.5% w/v yeast extract, 0.5% w/v NaCl, and 1% w/v tryptone) for placing into a 37.0 ℃ shaking incubator at 180 rpm for 12.0 h, respectively. Afterward, each bacterial solution was diluted to an appropriate dilution ratio, and 1.0 mL of each bacterial solution was poured into the PCA AGAR medium. At this time, the concentration of each bacterial solution was calculated to be ~ 108 CFU/mL. Next, 2.0 mL LB containing 1% (v/v) bacteria was added into a 24-well plate with the sterilized cell slides in each well and incubated in a 37.0 ℃ incubator overnight. After that, the culture medium was sucked out, and slowly washed with sterile PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 1 ×) three times. After that, 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs solution was added to the cell slides and respectively incubated for 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 h at 37.0 ℃ under dark conditions. Next, all solutions were sucked out and washed with sterile PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 1 ×) three times. Finally, bacteria on the cell slides were carried out fluorescence imaging on a fluorescence inverted microscope. The blue fluorescence images were captured at 420–460 nm with an excitation at 340–390 nm.

Both S. aureus and P. aeruginosa (~ 105 CFU/mL) were randomly divided into four groups: control group, only CS@Fe/CDs group, only H2O2 group, and CS@Fe/CDs plus H2O2 group, respectively. Among them, the control group received without any treatment, CS@Fe/CDs plus H2O2 group received treatment with 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs plus 500.0 μM H2O2 at 37.0 °C for 3.0 h, and other groups either received 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs or 500.0 μM H2O2 at 37.0 °C for 3.0 h. After that, all testing samples were transferred into LB medium to grow in a 37.0 °C incubator 18.0 h for observing bacteria survival state with eye-naked and used TEM to observe the morphology of bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular ROS generation within living bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Both S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were seeded on the sterilized cell slides and incubated according to the above method. At this point, both S. aureus and P. aeruginosa received four treatment methods, such as without any treatment, only 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs for 3.0 h incubation at 37.0 °C, only 500.0 μM H2O2 for incubation 3.0 h at 37.0 °C, and 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs together with 500.0 μM H2O2 at 37.0 °C for incubation 3.0 h, respectively. Subsequently, 330.0 μL, 10.0 μM DCFH-DA was incubated with each cell slide that adhered with bacteria for another 30.0 min under dark conditions. Next, each cell slide was washed with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 1 ×) three times to remove the floating dyes and bacteria. Finally, all bacteria on the cell slides were carried out fluorescence imaging on a fluorescence inverted microscope. The green fluorescence images were captured at 510–550 nm with an excitation at 470–495 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV staining assay for estimating bacterial biofilm biomass
ce:section.ce:para: 200.0 μL LB containing 1% (v/v) bacteria was added into a 96-well plate and incubated at 37.0 ℃ for 48.0 h, forming bacterial biofilms. For ensuring the formation of bacterial biofilms at the bottom of each well, the culture medium was carefully removed from each well and the bacteria adhered to each well were performed with sterile PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 1 ×) to slightly wash two times. Then, both S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms were randomly divided into four groups: control group (without any treatment), only CS@Fe/CDs group (50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs for treatment 3.0 h), only H2O2 group (500.0 μM H2O2 for treatment 3.0 h), and CS@Fe/CDs plus H2O2 group (50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs plus 500.0 μM H2O2 for 3.0 h), respectively. In this case, all samples were further placed into a 60.0 °C incubator for another 1.0 h incubation. Afterward, 200.0 μL PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 1 ×) containing CV (0.1 mg·mL−1) was added into each well for further 30.0 min incubation in dark. In turn, the PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 1 ×) was carefully removed from each well, and the 96-well plate was washed with sterile PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 1 ×) three times. Finally, the 96-well plate was naturally air-dried at room temperature, and then 200.0 μL 33% (v/v) ethanol solution was added into each well. Through measuring the absorbance of the product in each well at 590 nm on a microplate reader, the bacterial biofilm biomass could be estimated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial DNA damage assay within biofilm
ce:section.ce:para: Both the S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms were randomly divided into four groups: control (without any treatment), 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs, 500.0 μM H2O2, and 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs + 500.0 μM H2O2, respectively. After 12.0 h incubation at 37.0 °C, the Column Bacteria Genomic DNA Purification Kit was used to extract the DNA fragments from these treated S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms. At this point, a Nanodrop 2000 ultra-micro spectrophotometer was used to detect the residual levels of DNA for reflecting the relative levels of the eDNA inside S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms. Besides, 1% TAE agarose gel electrophoresis containing ethidium bromide staining was used to characterize the residual levels of DNA on the Gel Image system.

The sterilized cell slides adhered with S. aureus or P. aeruginosa biofilms were constructed in a 24-well plate by above method. Then, both S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms received four treatment methods, including control (without any treatment), only 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs for incubation 3.0 h at 37.0 °C, only 500.0 μM H2O2 at 37.0 °C for incubation 3.0 h, and 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs plus 500.0 μM H2O2 at 37.0 °C for incubation 3.0 h, respectively. For performing live/dead staining analysis, all above biofilms on the sterilized cell slides were stained with 50.0 μg·mL−1 SYTO-9 and 10.0 μg·mL−1 PI for 15.0 min under dark conditions. Finally, all the sterilized cell slides adhered with S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms were slightly washed with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 1 ×) three times for performing fluorescence imaging on a fluorescence imaging microscope. The green fluorescence images were gathered at 510–550 nm with an excitation at 470–495 nm, and the red fluorescence images were captured at 590–650 nm with an excitation at 540–580 nm. For performing intuitive SEM analysis, all above biofilms on the sterilized cell slides were treated with 2.5% glutaraldehyde fixative solution for fixing biofilms overnight at 4.0 °C. Afterward, all biofilms were washed with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 1 ×) for six times, which were then treated with 30%, 50%, 70%, and 90% absolute ethanol for dehydration in turn. Finally, the absolute ethanol was replaced with tert-butanol to further treat these biofilms for performing freeze-dried for 12.0 h. At this moment, all the sterilized cell slides adhered with biofilms were sprayed with gold to observe the morphological changes of bacterial biofilms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the synergistic antimicrobial activity of peroxidase mimic activity and CS, we firstly employed CS, citric acid, ethanediamine, and FeSO4.7 H2O as the raw materials to synthesize CS@Fe/CDs via one-pot hydrothermal method. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image revealed that CS@Fe/CDs was successfully synthesized, with a well dispersion ( Fig. 2a) and an average hydrodynamic diameter of ~ 15.69 nm (Fig. 2c). The lattice fringe of 1.6 Å on the surface of CS@Fe/CDs corresponded to the (100) plane-spacing graphite carbon with a good crystallinity (Inset of Fig. 2a) (Zhuo et al., 2019). In the XRD pattern, a broad peak with a center position at approximately 2θ = 21.36º indicated the presence of a large number of functional groups at the edges of the graphene layer both in CS@Fe/CDs and free Fe-doped CS@CDs (Fig. 2d) (Han et al., 2015). Compared with CS@CDs, the nanoparticle size of CS@Fe/CDs was obviously larger than that of CS@CDs (Fig. 2b), which was also verified by the small average hydrodynamic diameter of CS@CDs as ~ 3.12 nm (Fig. 2c). Meanwhile, we also noticed that the surface zeta potential of CS@Fe/CDs was 8.12 ± 0.62 mV, which was more positive than that of CS@CDs (2.47 ± 0.42 mV, Fig. 2e). These results probably implied the successful doping of Fe into CS@CDs for preparing CS@Fe/CDs. To prove this conjecture, we next carried out XPS analysis. As depicted in Fig. 2f, CS@Fe/CDs mainly included C, N, O and Fe elements, while Fe element was lacking in CS@CDs, clearly suggesting the successful doping of Fe into CS@CDs for yielding CS@Fe/CDs (Figs. S1 & S2). By further analyzing the Fe 2p spectrum, the characteristic peaks at ~ 710.55 eV and ~ 724 eV corresponded to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 states, revealing the existence of Fe2+. Moreover, we also noticed a weakly satellite peak at ~ 718 eV, which was originated from the Fe3+. However, the much larger area distribution of Fe2+ than that of Fe3+ demonstrated that Fe2+ was the dominant Fe element inside CS@Fe/CDs (Fig. 2g) (Sang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2021a; Zhang et al., 2022). Owing to the characteristic peak at ~ 401.12 eV in N 1 s spectrum, it could be convinced that there was sp2-hybridized N (C-NC) within CS@Fe/CDs. Compared with CS@CDs, the characteristic peak in CS@Fe/CDs was ~ 0.32 eV larger than that in CS@CDs (~ 400.80 eV). In contrast, the characteristic peak at ~ 399.69 eV assigned to tertiary N (N(C)3) both in CS@CDs and CS@Fe/CDs remained unchanged (Fig. 2h) (Zhang et al., 2016). These facts revealed that Fe primarily chelated with sp2-hybridized N (C-NC) within CS@CDs to acquire Fe-doped-carbon dots (CS@Fe/CDs). On this basis, we concluded that the initial characteristic peak of CS@CDs at ~ 234 nm red-shifted to ~ 247 nm in that of CS@Fe/CDs was also ascribable to Fe doping-triggered surface polarity and size increase (Shen et al., 2022, 2021). Furthermore, the absorption band of CS@Fe/CDs among 400–600 nm was stronger than that of CS@CDs in same conditions, revealing the change of metal-ligand covalent (Zimmer et al., 2018), which further proved the chelation of Fe with sp2-hybridized N inside CS@Fe/CDs (Fig. 2i).

Having confirmed the successful doping of Fe into CS@Fe/CDs, we subsequently investigated the grafting of CS on the surface of CS@Fe/CDs. In the FT-IR spectrum of CS, the characteristic peak at ~ 897 cm−1 assigned to β-1,4-glycosidic bonds, and other characteristic peaks at ~ 1152 cm−1 and ~ 1573 cm−1 corresponded to C-O-C bending and C-N stretching vibrations. Meanwhile, these characteristic peaks of CS were also observed both in the FT-IR spectra of CS@CDs and CS@Fe/CDs, while they not appeared in that of CS-free CDs, implying that the basic structure of CS was preserved inside CS@CDs and CS@Fe/CDs (Ji et al., 2017). Compared with CS, the absorption band of C-H vibration at 1064 – 1130 cm−1 both in the FT-IR spectra of CS@CDs and CS@Fe/CDs disappeared, which might result from CS chain degradation and the dehydration or decomposition of its pyran ring (Yang et al., 2012). Moreover, we noticed that the significant absorption peaks at ~ 3400 cm−1 and ~ 1668 cm−1 revealed the existence of –CONH both inside the FT-IR spectra of CS@CDs and CS@Fe/CDs ( Fig. 3a). According to these findings, we could be sure that CS was successfully grafted on the surface of CDs through covalent amide bond. In the absorption spectrum of CS@Fe/CDs, the characteristic absorption peak at ~ 247 nm was probably attributable to the π → π * transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, and the other absorption peak at ~ 365 nm might be due to the doped N atom-triggered surface excited defect states and CO caused n → π * transition (Fig. 3b) (Shen et al., 2020). Notably, CS@Fe/CDs possessed a maximum fluorescence emission at ~ 445 nm, which remained unchanged under different excitation wavelengths (Roy et al., 2015), further proving its rich-sp2 surface structure (Fig. 3c). Using quinine sulfate as standard fluorescence reference, we found that CS@Fe/CDs had a fluorescence quantum yield of ~ 28.09%, which could be acted as an effective candidate for fluorescent imaging (Fig. S3 & Note S1). In the view of the analysis above, we could be convinced that the successful doping of Fe and CS grafting made CS@Fe/CDs to be a competitive artificial nanozyme against bacteria fouling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Profiling the catalytic activity of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the successful doping of Fe into CS@Fe/CDs above, we then profiled its nanozyme catalytic activity. To this end, we first employed 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as an indicator to reveal the Fenton-like reaction catalytic activity of CS@Fe/CDs for probably inducing H2O2 to produce •OH. Generally, in the absence of •OH, TMB was colorless and exhibited insignificant absorption at ~ 652 nm. Once there was •OH, the colorless TMB could be oxidized into blue oxidation product ox-TMB (Fig. S4), resulting in a remarkable enhancement of absorption at ~ 652 nm (Niu et al., 2016). Based on this principle, we noticed that there were no obvious color and absorption changes for CS@Fe/CDs incubation with alone H2O2 or TMB, while the mixture of CS@Fe/CDs + H2O2 + TMB exhibited an obvious blue and significant absorption enhancement at ~ 652 nm. In the same experimental conditions, we found a negligible blue and absorption enhancement at ~ 652 nm in the mixture of Fe-free doping CS@CDs + H2O2 + TMB ( Fig. 4a). These findings suggested a good peroxidase mimic activity of CS@Fe/CDs, owing to the doped Fe in CS@CDs for triggering Fenton-like reaction. Considering the peroxidase mimic activity is closely related to the incubation pH and temperature, we next optimized the experimental conditions, such as pH, nanozyme concentrations, and temperature. Through performing colorimetric analysis, we found that the peroxidase mimic activity of CS@Fe/CDs was pH (Fig. 4b) and temperature dependent (Fig. 4c). In addition, when the nanozyme concentration was higher, its catalytic effect was better, which was consistent with the characteristics of natural enzymes (Fig. S5) (Ghavamipour et al., 2021). Afterward, we also examined the Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme by exploiting TMB and H2O2 as the substrates. It is well-known that the smaller K m value indicated the stronger affinity of enzyme for its substrates (Mafireyi et al., 2020). When H2O2 was used as the reaction substrate, the enzymatic parameter K m value was calculated from the Michaelis-Menten equation to be 0.156 mM, which was ~ 23.7-fold lower than that of natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP, 3.70 mM) (Figs. S6 & S7), suggesting that the catalytic activity of CS@Fe/CDs was much higher than that of HRP (Lin et al., 2014). More interestingly, as compared with the enzymatic parameter K m of other reported nanozymes, CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme showed a relatively higher affinity for both TMB and H2O2 (Table S1), proving a high peroxidase mimic activity of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme. It is reported that the rich-carboxyl groups on CS@Fe/CDs-based carbon nanozyme might be used as efficient binding sites of the substrates to capture H2O2, facilitating the electron transfer from CS@Fe/CDs to H2O2, thus increasing the electron density of active centers and reducing the steric site resistance to enhance its Fenton-like reaction-triggered peroxidase mimic activity (Song et al., 2010; Sun et al., 2015).

It was reported that •OH was capable of oxidizing terephthalic acid (TA) into 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH), which produced fluorescence emission at ~ 425 nm (Fig. S8) (Ge et al., 2014). Given such unique feature, we also performed the TA oxidation-based fluorescence assay to test the •OH generation from the Fenton-like reaction catalytic activity of CS@Fe/CDs. As shown in Figs. 4d & S9, only the group of CS@Fe/CDs together with H2O2 could cause TA oxidation to trigger a significant fluorescence enhancement at ~ 425 nm. However, neither CS@Fe/CDs and H2O2 leaded to this effect. These results clearly demonstrated the generation of •OH from the Fenton-like reaction catalytic activity of CS@Fe/CDs, agreeing well with the results of TMB-based colorimetric analysis above. To further confirm the •OH generation, methylene blue (MB) degradation-based colorimetric assay, and GSH oxidation-based Ellman’s assay were performed as well. The principle of MB degradation-based colorimetric assay was primarily attributable to •OH-caused absorption decrease of MB at ~ 665 nm (Wang and Huang, 2011). As illustrated in Figs. 4e & S10, there was a CS@Fe/CDs concentration-dependent absorption decrease of MB at ~ 665 nm, implying a high •OH generation capability of CS@Fe/CDs. Likewise, GSH oxidation-based Ellman’s assay usually employed 2-nitrobenzoic acid (DTNB) as indicator to monitor •OH-triggered change from yellow to colorless with an absorption decrease at ~ 410 nm (Yin et al., 2016). As expected, the characteristic absorption peak of GSH at ~ 410 nm significantly decreased due to the production of •OH caused by the Fenton-like reaction catalytic activity of CS@Fe/CDs, realizing GSH oxidation (Fig. S11). To more precisely determine the •OH, we employed 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as indicator to reveal the catalytic process through electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments. As illustrated in Fig. 4f, the mixture of CS@Fe/CDs with H2O2 could generate four peaks in the EPR spectrum, and the corresponding intensity ratio was about 1:2:2:1, proving that •OH was the main origin of ROS because of the DMPO-•OH generation (Chen et al., 2020). The above all experimental results strongly demonstrated that CS@Fe/CDs possessed an excellent peroxidase mimic activity to catalyze H2O2 into toxic •OH, foreboding a prominent antibacterial prospect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the antibacterial activity of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Have confirmed the excellent peroxidase mimic activity of CS@Fe/CDs, as a proof of concept, we further explored the antibacterial activity of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme on gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative P. aeruginosa. In this circumstance, we firstly exploited 50.0 μg·mL−1 of CS@Fe/CDs to incubate with living S. aureus and P. aeruginosa for 0.0 h, 0.5 h, 1.0 h, 2.0 h, and 3.0 h, respectively. After that, we performed the fluorescence imaging and observed that the maximum blue fluorescence of CS@Fe/CDs after 3.0 h incubation inside S. aureus (Fig. S12), while 1.0 h for P. aeruginosa (Fig. S13), indicating a good uptake of bacteria toward CS@Fe/CDs. By comparing with CS-free CDs, the blue fluorescence was very weak inside living P. aeruginosa even after 3.0 h incubation (Fig. S14). These facts were attributable to the binding of the CS on the surface of CS@Fe/CDs with the negatively charged cell membrane through electrostatic interaction, thus altering cell membrane permeability (Chung et al., 2008). Notably, there was a faster binding of CS@Fe/CDs toward P. aeruginosa than that of S. aureus. Two reasons might be on accounts for this phenomenon. On the one hand, the outer cell wall of gram-negative bacteria is abundant in lipopolysaccharides (LPS), which can be cross-linked with divalent cations through electrostatic interactions, while gram-positive bacteria are protected by a thicker peptidoglycan layer making the cell wall tough and dense (Dik et al., 2018; Ho et al., 2021). On the other hand, the hydrophilicity of gram-negative bacteria is higher than that of gram-positive bacteria and the amino groups present in the materials might have a higher affinity for the anionic free radicals present in the cell wall (Verlee et al., 2017). Therefore, gram-negative bacteria were more sensitive and easy binding to CS@Fe/CDs than gram-positive bacteria.

As a common ROS, H2O2 plays an important role in environmental microorganism disinfection and decontamination against microbial contamination for inducing infections. However, the high concentrations of H2O2 could be unavoidably harmful to the environment and industrial equipment in some extent as well (Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2021). Hence, it is of great practical significance for effectively killing bacteria with H2O2 at low levels as much as possible. In this regard, we examined the effects of H2O2 concentrations on the antibacterial activity of CS@Fe/CDs. The results indicated that the relative survival rates of both CS@Fe/CDs-treated living S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were progressively decreased with the increase of H2O2 levels. Only ~ 2.20% relative survival rate of S. aureus was observed at 600.0 μM H2O2, and only ~ 2.36% relative survival rate of P. aeruginosa was observed at 400.0 μM H2O2 (Fig. S15). On this basis, in the presence of 500.0 μM H2O2, we also noticed that only ~ 4.80% relative survival rate of S. aureus was observed at 60.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs, and only ~ 5.39% relative survival rate of P. aeruginosa was observed at 40.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs (Fig. S16). Apart from these, we further observed that there were concentration-dependent antibacterial activities both of alone CS@Fe/CDs (Fig. S17) and alone CS@CDs (Fig. S18), while there was a negligible antibacterial activity of alone CDs (Fig. S19). At this point, we exploited 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) as a fluorescence indicator to compare the catalytic activities of CS@Fe/CDs, CS@CDs, and CDs in same conditions. The results revealed that neither CS@CDs nor CDs failed to catalyze H2O2 for producing •OH, because of the insignificant fluorescence change of DCFH-DA. In contrast, CS@Fe/CDs could trigger H2O2 to produce •OH for converting DCFH-DA into DCF with strong fluorescence (Guo et al., 2020) (Fig. S20). All of these findings clearly suggested the antimicrobial activity of CS inside CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme. Like to the imaging results, the antibacterial activity of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme against P. aeruginosa was better than that of S. aureus at the same experimental condition. Based on the above results, we chose 50.0 μg·mL−1 CS@Fe/CDs and 500.0 μM H2O2 to incubate with bacteria at 3.0 h for measuring the next antibacterial experiments with eye-naked. As displayed in Fig. 5a to c, the mortality rates of alone CS@Fe/CDs alone against S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were ~ 17.3% and ~ 22.5%, respectively. However, in the presence of H2O2, both S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were almost completely killed, proving the synergistic antibacterial effects of CS and peroxidase-like catalytic activity inside CS@Fe/CDs.

To verify the antibacterial mechanism of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme, we observed the bacterial morphology through TEM images with different treatments. As exhibited in Fig. 5d, the morphologies of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa showed slight deformation following incubation with alone CS@Fe/CDs. Moreover, the cell membrane of S. aureus ruptured to some extent, and the surface of P. aeruginosa became broken to cause no longer smooth, which might derive from the synergistic effects of CS natural antibacterial activity together with CS@Fe/CDs-triggered •OH from trace amounts of H2O2 in bacterial microenvironment through Fenton-like reaction. After receiving the simultaneous treatment with CS@Fe/CDs and H2O2, the cell membranes of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa completely ruptured to cause remarkable necrotic lesions and fragmentary. To validate •OH generation inside living bacteria, DCFH-DA was employed as an indicator to carry out •OH fluorescence imaging in living S. aureus and P. aeruginosa. As shown in Fig. 5e & S21, the living bacteria only treated with CS@Fe/CDs plus H2O2 exhibited strong green fluorescence signals by comparing to the treatment with alone CS@Fe/CDs or alone H2O2, clearly proving the efficient production of •OH from CS@Fe/CDs-triggered Fenton-like reaction in living bacteria. Of particular mention was that the green fluorescence signals inside P. aeruginosa were stronger than that inside S. aureus, revealing that CS@Fe/CDs possessed a higher capability to catalyze •OH generation within P. aeruginosa than that within S. aureus. Combined with the previous imaging results, CS@Fe/CDs was capable of producing more •OH under the same experimental conditions due to its ability to bind to P. aeruginosa faster and more effectively. These findings strongly illustrated that this designed CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme had obviously synergistic antibacterial effects through CS and the high peroxide activity inside CS@Fe/CDs to catalyze H2O2 for producing toxic •OH to make the bacterial cell membrane rupture, thus causing the remarkable bacteria death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial biofilm eradication using CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Having revealed the excellent antibacterial activity of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme above, we finally explored its antibiofilm capability. Regarding this point, we first adopted classical crystal violet (CV) staining assay to prove the formation of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms on sterilized cell slides after incubating at 37.0 ℃ for 48.0 h (Fig. S22). After that, the above bacterial biofilms received treatments without or with CS@Fe/CDs (50.0 μg·mL−1), H2O2 (500.0 μM), and CS@Fe/CDs (50.0 μg·mL−1) plus H2O2 (500.0 μM) at 37.0 ºC for 3.0 h, respectively. For visual observation of these bioﬁlms, we performed the live/dead bacteria staining experiments with two kinds of fluorescent nucleic acid dyes SYTO-9/PI. It is well-known that SYTO-9 could enter all bacterial cells to bind DNA for emitting green fluorescence, while PI could only penetrate into dead bacterial cells to emit red fluorescence, causing the remarkable fluorescence decrease of SYTO-9 when both dyes are present inside dead bacterial cells (Wang et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 6a, we found that weak red fluorescence signals and bright green fluorescence signals were observed in the bacterial biofilms treated with alone CS@Fe/CDs and alone H2O2. In contrast, both S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms treated with CS@Fe/CDs plus H2O2 showed bright red fluorescence signals and almost insignificant green fluorescence signals. By using DCFH-DA to perform the colocalization analysis, we noticed that the •OH was primarily generated inside the biofilms, owing to the most of DCF green fluorescence overlapping with CS@Fe/CDs blue fluorescence (Fig. S23). These facts implied that the Fenton-like reaction catalytic activity of CS@Fe/CDs could effectively induce H2O2 to produce toxic •OH into bacterial biofilms, thus causing remarkably bacterial biofilm destruction. Moreover, the similar results were intuitively observed by monitoring biofilm morphology on thermal field scanning electron microscope (SEM) images. From the experimental results, we noticed that the surface both of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms receiving treatment with CS@Fe/CDs or H2O2 could maintain smoothness and integrity. Different from this, the bacterial cell membrane structure both in S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms receiving treatment with CS@Fe/CDs plus H2O2 were obviously destroyed, thus causing the remarkable surface shrink and rupture (Fig. 6b). Through the quantitative analysis of CV staining, over ~ 95.28% of P. aeruginosa biofilm biomass treated with CS@Fe/CDs plus H2O2 was scavenged, while ~ 83.23% of S. aureus biofilm biomass was reduced in the same conditions (Figs. 6c & S24). These findings further confirmed that gram-negative bacteria were more sensitive to the effects of CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozymes. The results displayed that the biofilm elimination capacity results were similar to the antibacterial activity. Therefore, the CS@Fe/CDs had a higher binding speed and affinity with P. aeruginosa may cause the more effective antibiofilm activity through the combination with the natural antibacterial activity of CS and the producing of highly toxic •OH.

As previously reported, the resistance of bacterial biofilms mainly originated from the special EPS, consisting of proteins, polysaccharides, and eDNA. Among them, eDNA is regarded as a “crosslinking agent” to assemble bacteria and other components of EPS into complete biofilms. Meanwhile, eDNA is positively related to bacterial adhesion and the stability of bacterial biofilms (Guo et al., 2020; Haubenreisser et al., 2016). Driven by these points, in combination with the results of the remarkably decreased green fluorescence signals of SYTO-9 inside S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms above, we speculated that CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme could catalyze H2O2 to generate •OH for initiating efficient eDNA cleavage surrounding biofilm microenvironment, thereby playing an important role in disrupting bacterial biofilms. Regarding this, both the living S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms were first incubated without or with CS@Fe/CDs (50.0 μg·mL−1), H2O2 (500.0 μM), and CS@Fe/CDs (50.0 μg·mL−1) plus H2O2 (500.0 μM) at 37.0 ºC for 12.0 h, respectively. After that, we utilized the agarose gel electrophoresis to assess the residual levels of DNA inside these treated biofilms for reflecting the eDNA levels. As depicted in Fig. 6d & e, the DNA fragments both from living S. aureus and P. aeruginosa biofilms had virtually no damage with alone CS@Fe/CDs or alone H2O2. However, these DNA fragments were almost completely degraded with CS@Fe/CDs plus H2O2. Likewise, the residual levels of DNA in the biofilms receiving the treatment of CS@Fe/CDs plus H2O2 was obviously lower than that of other groups (Fig. S25). These findings strongly proved that CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme possessed the antibiofilm ability was mainly attributable to its Fenton-like reaction-triggered peroxidase-like catalytic activity for toxic •OH generation, causing effective eDNA cleavage surrounding biofilm microenvironment (Chen et al., 2016; Guo et al., 2020).

In conclusion, we have firstly exploited one-pot hydrothermal method to successfully synthesize CS@Fe/CDs, which showed an excellent peroxidase-like activity to catalyze the production of •OH through Fenton-like reaction. Experimental results revealed that the peroxidase-like activity was much better than that of natural HRP. By integrating with the synergistic antibiofilm activity of CS, the peroxidase-like activity of CS@Fe/CDs made it to ablate bacterial biofilms via cleaving eDNA. As a proof of concept, CS@Fe/CDs has exhibited a high efficiency in treating gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative P. aeruginosa biofilms. In a word, this work presented a superior synergistic strategy for efficient and cost-effective eradication of bacterial biofilms. We believe that this CS@Fe/CDs-based nanozyme with synergistic antibiofilm effect not only gives valuable information for the development of efficient biofilm treatment, but also provides new insights into the extensively investigated for development of economical and eco-friendly CDs-based nanozymes in safeguarding food safety and environmental health.

Ting Pan: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Huanhuan Chen: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiang Gao: Formal analysis. Zeyu Wu: Formal analysis. Yingwang Ye: Formal analysis, Resources, Supervision. Yizhong Shen: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
